message.content:
You are an adversarial skeptic. Try to REFUTE this pass-10 finding about /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay by reading the actual code. REFUTED if false, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design (dataset-agnostic engine, optional import-guarded backends, intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts, on-box data-sovereignty, documented honest-scope), or not a real production defect. Default to REFUTED if it doesn't hold. Use python/pytest/gh as needed.

FINDING P10-CI-1 (high, CIEXEC): require_green_ci.sh pre-merge gate never compares the CI run's commit SHA to HEAD — certifies merges for untested commits (stale-run hole)
Location: scripts/require_green_ci.sh:22-33
Claim: The compensating pre-merge gate that substitutes for unenforceable branch protection (#CI-1) queries only `gh run list --branch "$branch" --workflow ci.yml --limit 1` and checks that run's status/conclusion. It never reads or compares the run's `headSha` against the local/remote tip it is about to merge. So the gate reports 'CI is green — safe to merge' based on whatever the most-recent run for the branch was, regardless of which commit that run tested.
Why real: Concrete, reproducible failure mode: (1) push commit A, CI goes green; (2) add a NEW commit B (or amend/force-push) on the same branch; (3) run `scripts/require_green_ci.sh` before CI for B has started (or while B's run is still in_progress so the latest *completed* success is still A's). `gh run list --limit 1` returns A's green run, the script prints 'safe to merge' and exits 0, and the operator merges B — code CI never ran on. I confirmed the exact mechanism: `gh run list --branch main --workflow ci.yml` exposes a per-run `headSha`, and right now the local pass-10 HEAD is 17b4eb52 while `require_green_ci.sh main` would certify green off run a49b3f68 — a DIFFERENT commit. This is precisely the un-gated-red-merge class the script was written to prevent (the doc admits CI 'went silently red across several hardening passes'), but the implementation leaves the door open for the stale-/wrong-SHA case. Branch protection that would normally enforce SHA association is unavailable on this plan, so this script is the ONLY gate.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll examine the actual script to verify this finding.

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# Pre-merge CI gate (#CI-1) — compensating control for an unenforceable required status check.
3	#
4	# Branch protection / required status checks are UNAVAILABLE on this repo's plan: it is private on
5	# a free tier, so `gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>/branches/main/protection` returns HTTP 403
6	# ("Upgrade to GitHub Pro or make this repository public"). GitHub therefore does NOT block merging
7	# a PR whose CI is red — the `all-checks` fan-in job is computed but nothing consumes it as a gate.
8	# That is the exact mechanism by which CI went silently red across several hardening passes (merged
9	# via --admin / un-gated). Making the repo public to enable protection is out of scope (it would
10	# publish the committed transcripts); upgrading the plan is an operator/billing decision.
11	#
12	# Until one of those changes, THIS is the gate: it exits non-zero unless the latest `ci` run for the
13	# branch concluded success. Run it before every merge and refuse to merge on failure:
14	#
15	#   scripts/require_green_ci.sh [branch]      # defaults to the current branch
16	#   scripts/require_green_ci.sh && gh pr merge <n> --merge --delete-branch
17	set -euo pipefail
18	
19	branch="${1:-$(git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD)}"
20	read -r status conclusion < <(
21	  gh run list --branch "$branch" --workflow ci.yml --limit 1 \
22	    --json status,conclusion -q '.[0].status + " " + (.[0].conclusion // "none")'
23	)
24	echo "latest ci run for '$branch': status=$status conclusion=$conclusion"
25	if [ "$status" = "completed" ] && [ "$conclusion" = "success" ]; then
26	  echo "CI is green — safe to merge."
27	  exit 0
28	fi
29	echo "::error::CI for '$branch' is not green (status=$status conclusion=$conclusion) — do NOT merge (#CI-1)." >&2
30	exit 1
31

content.text:
The finding is confirmed by the code. The script queries `gh run list --branch "$branch" --workflow ci.yml --limit 1` and only extracts `status,conclusion` (lines 20-23). It never reads `headSha` nor compares it against the tip being merged.

Let me verify the claimed reproducible mechanism with the actual repo state.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git rev-parse HEAD && git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && gh run list --branch main --workflow ci.yml --limit 3 --json status,conclusion,headSha,createdAt 2>&1 | head -50
input.description: List recent CI runs with headSha

content.text:
The mechanism is confirmed empirically. The latest completed `main` CI run has `headSha=a49b3f68...`, and the script extracts only `.[0].status` and `.[0].conclusion` — so `require_green_ci.sh main` would print "CI is green — safe to merge" based on commit a49b3f68, while certifying a merge for any other tip (the finding cites pass-10 HEAD 17b4eb52 as a different commit).

Now let me check whether this gate is truly the only enforcement, and whether there's any SHA check elsewhere in the merge path that mitigates it.

input.description: Search for any SHA comparison or callers of the gate

content.content:
scripts/require_green_ci.sh:15:#   scripts/require_green_ci.sh [branch]      # defaults to the current branch
scripts/require_green_ci.sh:16:#   scripts/require_green_ci.sh && gh pr merge <n> --merge --delete-branch
scripts/require_green_ci.sh:21:  gh run list --branch "$branch" --workflow ci.yml --limit 1 \
.github/workflows/ci.yml:232:  # protection, the compensating control is `scripts/require_green_ci.sh` (run before every merge)

content.text:
Confirmed. There is no SHA comparison anywhere in the merge path. `require_green_ci.sh` is the sole compensating gate (branch protection is 403/unavailable per the script's own header and ci.yml:232), and it:

1. Queries only `--limit 1` most-recent run by status/conclusion.
2. Never reads `headSha`.
3. Never compares against `git rev-parse HEAD` / the remote tip being merged.

The reproducible stale-/wrong-SHA hole is real: when a newer commit's run hasn't started or is still in-progress, `--limit 1` returns the prior green run and the script exits 0 ("safe to merge"). I empirically confirmed the latest green `main` run is for a49b3f68, a commit distinct from the current local pass-10 HEAD 17b4eb52, exactly as the finding states.

This is not out-of-scope: it is a security/correctness defect in the *only* pre-merge gate, defeating the very un-gated-red-merge class the script exists to prevent. The proper fix is to fetch `headSha` for the latest run and require it equals the tip being certified (and reject `in_progress`/missing runs for the target SHA rather than falling back to an older run).

The finding stands.

input.reasoning:
Verified against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/require_green_ci.sh (lines 17-30). The gate runs `gh run list --branch "$branch" --workflow ci.yml --limit 1 --json status,conclusion -q '.[0].status + " " + (.[0].conclusion // "none")'` and decides solely on status==completed && conclusion==success. It never extracts or compares the run's headSha against the tip being merged (no `git rev-parse`, no headSha read anywhere in the merge path — grep over scripts/ and .github/ confirms only this single un-SHA-checked query exists).

The reproducible stale-/wrong-SHA mechanism is real and I confirmed it empirically: `gh run list --branch main --workflow ci.yml --json status,conclusion,headSha` returns the latest completed run as conclusion=success with headSha=a49b3f685c..., while the local pass-10 HEAD is 17b4eb5279... (branch harden/pass-10). So `require_green_ci.sh main` would print "CI is green — safe to merge" / exit 0 based on a49b3f68's run, certifying a merge of a DIFFERENT commit that CI never validated. The classic failure flow holds: push A (green), add/amend B, run the gate before B's run completes — `--limit 1` returns A's success and the gate green-lights B.

Not refutable on scope grounds: the script's own header and ci.yml:232 establish that branch protection is unavailable (HTTP 403 on a private free-tier repo) and that this script is the ONLY compensating gate against the un-gated-red-merge class that previously let CI go silently red across passes. A gate that certifies untested commits defeats its sole purpose. This is a genuine high-severity production defect in the merge-safety control. Correct fix: pull the latest run's headSha and require it equals the local/remote tip being merged, and treat in_progress/absent runs for the target SHA as non-green rather than falling back to an older success.

content.text: Finding P10-CI-1 STANDS — verified against the actual code and empirically reproduced. The gate decides on status/conclusion of the single most-recent run and never compares headSha to the tip being merged; the latest green `main` run (a49b3f68) is a different commit than current HEAD (17b4eb52), so the gate would falsely certify untested commits. It is the sole pre-merge control (branch protection is 403-unavailable), so the hole is fully exposed.
